items.q: What are the units of Kc for a reaction?
items.correct: They depend on Δn (the difference between moles of gaseous/aqueous products and reactants) — Kc can be dimensionless or have units like mol dm^-3.
items.misconception: Kc always has units of mol dm^-3.
items.why: Students learn Kc with a fixed unit example and don't realise units arise from the exponents in the expression; for A + B ⇌ C + D, Kc is unitless, but for A ⇌ B + C, Kc has units.

items.q: Why does benzene undergo substitution rather than addition reactions?
items.correct: Adding across a C=C would destroy the stable delocalised pi system; substitution preserves the aromatic ring.
items.misconception: Benzene contains three normal alternating double bonds and behaves like an alkene with three C=C.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react at all because it's too stable.
items.why: The Kekule diagram shows fixed double bonds, so students assume benzene should behave like cyclohexatriene — missing the resonance/delocalisation that changes its reactivity.

items.q: Can a single standard electrode potential be measured in isolation?
items.correct: No — electrode potentials are RELATIVE values measured against a reference (the standard hydrogen electrode, defined as 0 V).
items.misconception: Each electrode has an absolute potential that can be measured directly with a voltmeter.
items.random_wrong: Standard electrode potentials are measured by submerging the electrode in pure water.
items.why: Students think a voltmeter can give a single electrode's value; they don't grasp that potential difference requires two electrodes by definition.

items.q: Which species has the same electron configuration as Ar?
items.why: It's Cl⁻. “Na⁺” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Successive ionisation energies of an atom always:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Increase. “Decrease” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
